verses.commentary: Bear with (bastazō, βαστάζω) means carry, endure. Failings (asthenēmata, ἀσθενήματα) are weaknesses, scruples. Not pleasing ourselves: self-denial for others' sake. Strength is for service, not self-indulgence.
Please (areskō, ἀρέσκω) the neighbor. The purpose: their good and building up (oikodomē, οἰκοδομή, edification). Love seeks the other's benefit, not one's own preferences.
Christ is the supreme example. He did not please Himself. He bore reproach for God's sake. Insults directed at God fell on Christ. If Christ denied Himself, how much more should we.
Endurance (hypomonē, ὑπομονή) and encouragement (paraklēsis, παράκλησις) from Scripture produce hope. The Old Testament is for our benefit. Scripture sustains hope through trials.
The petition: unity of mind (to auto phroneō, τὸ αὐτὸ φρονέω) according to Christ Jesus. Christ's mindset (humility, self-denial) should mark believers. Unity flows from shared Christlikeness.
One mind (homothumadon, ὁμοθυμαδόν, with one accord) and one voice (one mouth) glorify God. Unity magnifies God's glory. Divisions hinder worship.
The ground: Christ accepted us. The purpose: God's glory. Christ's acceptance of us, while we were sinners, models how we accept others.

Christ's mission to Jews: servant (diakonos, διάκονος) on behalf of God's truth. Purpose: confirm the promises to Abraham, Isaac, Jacob. God's faithfulness to Israel is demonstrated in Christ. The Messiah fulfills covenant promises.
Christ's mission also includes Gentiles. They glorify God for His mercy (eleos, ἔλεος). Old Testament prophecy (Psalm 18:49) foretold Gentile worship. The gospel extends beyond Israel to all nations.
A second quotation. Gentiles rejoice with God's people (Israel). This anticipates the unity of Jew and Gentile in one body. The Old Testament envisioned this inclusion.
A third quotation. All Gentiles are called to praise. All peoples (panta ta ethnē, πάντα τὰ ἔθνη) extol God. This is universal worship. God's glory extends to every nation.
A fourth quotation. The Root of Jesse (Messiah from David's line) will rule the nations (Gentiles). Gentiles will hope in Him. Christ is the fulfillment: King over all nations, hope for all peoples.
God of hope grants joy and peace through trust. The result: overflowing hope by the Spirit's power. Hope, joy, and peace are Spirit-given realities. Trust in God produces abundant hope.

Full of goodness (agathōsynē, ἀγαθωσύνη), filled with knowledge (gnōsis, γνῶσις), and competent to instruct (noutheteō, νουθετέω, admonish). This is pastoral affirmation. Believers can teach and correct each other.
Paul wrote boldly (tolmēroterōs, τολμηρότερως, more daringly) as a reminder. The basis: grace given him (apostolic calling). Paul's boldness flows from his commission, not arrogance.

Priestly duty (hierourgeō, ἱερουργέω, priestly service) of proclaiming the gospel. The Gentiles are an offering (prosphora, προσφορά) to God, sanctified by the Spirit. Paul's ministry is priestly: presenting converted Gentiles as a sacrifice to God.
us, not himself. His service (ta pros ton theon, τὰ πρὸς τὸν θεόν, things pertaining to God) is through Christ. All ministry glory belongs to Christ.
Leading Gentiles to obedience (hypakoē, ὑπακοή) is the goal. Word and deed (speech and action) both matter. Ministry success is Christ's work, not Paul's.
Spirit's power enabled ministry. Geographic scope: Jerusalem to Illyricum (modern Balkans). Fully proclaimed (plēroō, πληρόω) means completed, fulfilled. Paul's missionary reach was vast.
Preach where Christ is not known. Not building on another's foundation respects others' work and extends gospel reach. Paul was a church planter, not a settler.

Old Testament warrant for Paul's mission. Those not told will see; those not hearing will understand. This justifies reaching unreached peoples. God's plan includes taking the gospel to those who have never heard.
His calling to unreached areas kept him from visiting established churches. Priorities shaped his travel.
No more place means no more pioneering opportunities in that region. Paul's longstanding desire: visit Rome. Timing and calling align.

Assist (propempō, προπέμπω) means send on one's way with provision. Paul hopes for Roman support for Spain. After enjoying fellowship, he will continue west. Fellowship and mission are both priorities.
Service (diakoneō, διακονέω, minister) to the Lord's people. This refers to the collection from Gentile churches for Jewish believers (Acts 24:17). Unity is expressed through generosity.
Pleased (eudokeō, εὐδοκέω) indicates willing generosity. The poor (ptōchoi, πτωχοί) in Jerusalem needed support. Gentile believers helped Jewish believers.
Gentiles received spiritual blessings (the gospel) from Jews. Jews should receive material blessings from Gentiles. This reciprocal relationship expresses unity.
Made sure (sphragizō, σφραγίζω, sealed, secured) suggests safe delivery. Paul's plans are detailed and purposeful.
Full measure (plērōma, πλήρωμα) suggests abundant blessing. Apostolic presence brings Christ's blessing to the church.

By Christ and by the Spirit's love, Paul urges prayer. Join in my struggle (synagōnizomai, συναγωνίζομαι, strive together with) depicts partnership. Prayer is spiritual warfare. Paul needs the Romans' intercession.
Both are real concerns. Not all Jewish believers trusted Paul or welcomed Gentile support.
Be refreshed (synanapauomai, συναναπαύομαι, rest together with) suggests mutual encouragement. Paul longs for fellowship with the Romans. Ministry is sustained by Christian community.
The God of peace (eirēnē, εἰρήνη, shalom) grants wholeness. With you all is comprehensive. Amen (so be it) seals the blessing.
